title: The Unsent Message
theme: Liam wants connection but has spent years in an environment where emotions are rarely discussed. He carries the habit of silence with him everywhere.

lines.speaker: narration
lines.text: INT. LIAM'S BEDROOM — 7:02 AM. The room is quiet. A desk covered in textbooks. A neatly made bed. Everything looks organized. Almost too organized.

lines.speaker: narration
lines.text: LIAM, 13, sits at his desk. School uniform already on. Backpack packed. Ready to leave. But he isn't moving. He's staring at his phone.

lines.speaker: avery
lines.text: People think loneliness always starts when nobody wants you around. Sometimes it starts way before that.

messages.text: we should probably meet after school

pause_dialogue.speaker: player
pause_dialogue.text: Okay. What's the problem? They literally asked him a question.

pause_dialogue.speaker: avery
pause_dialogue.text: Exactly. So why hasn't he answered?

pause_dialogue.speaker: narration
pause_dialogue.text: Player clicks Liam's phone. A reply box appears. 'yeah sounds good' — the message is typed. Ready to send. But unsent.

pause_dialogue.speaker: player
pause_dialogue.text: Wait. He's already written the reply.

pause_dialogue.speaker: narration
pause_dialogue.text: New thoughts appear. Maybe that's too eager. Maybe they don't actually want me there. Maybe everyone already knows each other. Maybe I should wait.

pause_dialogue.speaker: avery
pause_dialogue.text: For some people, four words can feel like four hundred.

dialogue.speaker: avery
dialogue.text: Sending it was the easy part. Now he has to actually show up.

outcome.insight: Taking the first step — even a small one — is harder than it looks when fear of judgment is involved.

choices.text: yeah sounds good if that's okay

messages.text: yeah sounds good if that's okay

dialogue.speaker: player
dialogue.text: 'If that's okay.' Like he needs permission to be included.

dialogue.speaker: avery
dialogue.text: When you've spent a long time feeling like a burden, you start asking for permission to exist in spaces.

outcome.insight: Hedging language — 'if that's okay', 'if you don't mind' — can reveal a deep fear of taking up space.

dialogue.speaker: avery
dialogue.text: He knows. That's what makes the silence louder.

outcome.insight: Avoidance feels like relief in the moment — but it reinforces the belief that connection is dangerous.

scenes.prompt: Watch Liam move through the hallway.

dialogue.speaker: player
dialogue.text: Hold on. Did he just take the longer hallway?

dialogue.speaker: avery
dialogue.text: He keeps choosing routes that avoid people.

dialogue.speaker: player
dialogue.text: That's a lot of effort to avoid nothing.

dialogue.speaker: avery
dialogue.text: It doesn't feel like nothing to him.

dialogue.speaker: player
dialogue.text: So why does he look at them like they're a problem?

dialogue.speaker: avery
dialogue.text: Because he doesn't know what to say next. And the silence feels like a trap.

dialogue.speaker: narration
dialogue.text: A student waves. ETHAN: Hey Liam.

dialogue.speaker: narration
dialogue.text: Liam gives a small nod. Keeps walking.

dialogue.speaker: avery
dialogue.text: You assume he knows what to say next.

closing.speaker: narration
closing.text: Liam reaches his classroom. Sits at the back. Opens his textbook before anyone can start a conversation.

closing.speaker: player
closing.text: He's really good at making himself invisible.

lines.speaker: narration
lines.text: INT. DINING ROOM — EVENING. Liam sits with his parents. Dinner is quiet. Not hostile. Just distant.

lines.speaker: player
lines.text: Nobody asked if he was okay.

lines.speaker: narration
lines.text: LIAM: We got project groups today.

lines.speaker: narration
lines.text: DAD: Good. Group work teaches responsibility.

lines.speaker: narration
lines.text: LIAM: One of the guys invited me to football practice.

lines.speaker: narration
lines.text: DAD: Just make sure it doesn't affect your grades.

lines.speaker: narration
lines.text: Liam finishes eating. Nobody asks how he feels. Nobody asks if he's made friends. Nobody asks if he's happy. He goes to his room.

lines.speaker: narration
lines.text: Back in his room. Liam opens his phone. The project group chat again.

lines.speaker: narration
lines.text: Liam stares at it. The first genuine smile all chapter.

lines.speaker: narration
lines.text: Liam begins typing. Stops. Deletes. Types again. Deletes again. Locks the phone.

lines.speaker: player
lines.text: Even after proof they want him there?

lines.speaker: avery
lines.text: You can spend years learning that your feelings don't matter. One text message doesn't undo that overnight.

lines.speaker: avery
lines.text: Tonight? Nothing. But maybe tomorrow is different.

lines.speaker: avery
lines.text: Sometimes loneliness isn't about being excluded. Sometimes it's about standing at the door... and never feeling able to walk through it.

scenes.title: The Thought Filter
scenes.instructions: Liam's thoughts appear one by one. Sort each one — is it Worth Sending or Overthinking?

thoughts.explanation: Eagerness is not a flaw. Wanting to connect is natural — the fear of appearing too eager is the distortion.

thoughts.text: They asked me a direct question.
thoughts.explanation: This is observable and true. They reached out. That is real evidence of inclusion.

thoughts.text: Maybe they don't actually want me there.
thoughts.explanation: There is no evidence for this. It is fear speaking, not reality.
A direct, warm response. Someone is expecting him. That is fact, not assumption.

thoughts.text: Everyone already knows each other and I'll be left out.
thoughts.explanation: This is a prediction about the future, not a fact about the present. Most people at new social situations feel the same way.

thoughts.text: Ethan waved at me in the hallway.
thoughts.explanation: Someone acknowledged him. That is a real social signal — one Liam tends to dismiss.

thoughts.text: If I say the wrong thing they'll think I'm weird.
thoughts.explanation: This is catastrophising — imagining the worst outcome from a simple interaction.

thoughts.text: They included me in the project group.
thoughts.explanation: He was chosen to be part of something. That is evidence of belonging, not rejection.

scenes.title: What He Could Have Said
scenes.instructions: These are real moments from Liam's day. Choose the response that opens a door instead of closing one.

situations.context: In the school hallway, Ethan waves and says:

choices.outcome: Ethan watches him go. The moment passes. Another small door closes.

choices.text: Hey! You going to the library after school?
choices.outcome: Ethan smiles. 'Yeah actually. See you there?' A real exchange. A small connection made.
Ethan looks confused. Liam spends the next hour wondering if Ethan thinks he's rude.
Dad nods. Conversation ends. Another evening passes in silence.

choices.text: Actually kind of weird. We got project groups and I wasn't sure if I was wanted.
choices.outcome: Dad puts his fork down. 'What do you mean?' For the first time, dinner becomes a real conversation.
Vague enough to require no follow-up. Safe. Empty.

situations.context: In the group chat, after 12 minutes of silence, Jack asks:

choices.outcome: Simple. Direct. The group moves forward. Liam is part of it.
The group assumes he's busy. They plan without him. He watches from outside.

choices.text: i can come if you actually need me there
choices.outcome: Jack reads it twice. 'Why wouldn't we need you?' The self-doubt is visible now — to everyone.

situations.context: After Emma says 'awesome see u tomorrow :)', Liam types and deletes. Finally he could send:

choices.outcome: Emma waits. Then assumes he saw it. A small uncertainty grows where certainty could have been.
Two words. But enough. Emma smiles. Liam stares at his screen. For a moment, the room feels less quiet.

choices.text: yeah if I can make it
choices.outcome: He was already confirmed. This undoes it. Emma sends a question mark. Now there's a conversation he has to manage.

scenes.title: Chapter 1 — Liam Complete
scenes.summary: Liam wasn't excluded. He was invited, waved at, included in a group chat, confirmed for tomorrow. But none of it reached him — because somewhere along the way he learned that his feelings weren't worth sharing. That his presence needed permission. That silence was safer than risk. Loneliness isn't always about being locked outside. Sometimes it's never learning how to walk through an open door.
keyLearnings: Overthinking can make a four-word reply feel impossible.
Fear of judgment is not the same as actual rejection.
A quiet family environment can teach children that emotions are not worth sharing.
Small moments of connection — a wave, a reply, a smile — matter more than they seem.
Loneliness can grow even when surrounded by people who are trying to include you.
